votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification by filtering out all vowel characters from the input string and comparing the result.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification by filtering out vowels from the input string and comparing the result.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification by filtering out all vowels from the input string and comparing it to the result.

Both implementations check if each character is a vowel (case-insensitive for a, e, i, o, u) and filter out vowels from the input text. The Coq code uses existsb with a list of vowel characters and filter with negb, while Python uses 'in' membership check and a generator with 'not'. Both produce the same filtered string for any concrete input.

Both implementations check if each character is a vowel (case-insensitive for a,e,i,o,u) and filter out vowels from the input string. The Coq code uses existsb with Ascii.eqb to check membership in the vowel list, while Python uses 'in' operator - both produce the same result. The filtering logic (negb in Coq, 'not' in Python) and string reconstruction are equivalent for all concrete string inputs.

Both implementations check if each character is a vowel (case-insensitive for a, e, i, o, u) and filter out vowels from the input string. The Coq code uses is_vowel with existsb over a list of vowel characters, and filters with negb. The Python code uses 'in' to check membership in the same vowel list and filters with 'not is_vowel(c)'. Both produce the same filtered string for any concrete input.
Both define vowels as a/e/i/o/u in both cases and remove them from the input string; the Python code filters characters identically to the Coq spec and compares the result to this expected string.
Both implementations remove only the characters a, e, i, o, u (case-insensitive) from the input and compare the resulting string to result, yielding the same outcome on all concrete inputs.
Both implementations remove characters in {a,e,i,o,u,A,E,I,O,U} and keep all others, and the Python function checks result equality accordingly.
